Q1: Honestly, my study routine was chaotic at best. I'd go through phases where I'd binge-watch lecture videos for hours, then completely disappear for weeks. With ten online courses, I couldn't maintain any real structure—I just put out whatever fire was burning brightest. Most of my actual studying happened in intense bursts, usually late at night when anxiety kept me awake anyway.
Q2: My pattern was entirely deadline-driven, no regularity whatsoever. I'd check when assignments were due and work backward from there, cramming everything into the last possible window. Some weeks I'd study twenty hours, others barely three. Life stuff kept interfering—family obligations, my own mental health spiraling—so consistency was a luxury I couldn't afford.
Q3: Usually panic prompted me to log in. I'd suddenly remember an assignment or realize I'd missed something important. Sometimes I'd get an email notification about a deadline and that would jolt me into action. Occasionally I logged in out of guilt, just to see how far behind I'd fallen, which honestly made the anxiety worse.
Q4: Not really, no. The LMS never became routine for me—it was more like an emergency room I visited during crises. There were brief periods where I tried to check in daily, but that fell apart within a week. The only consistent thing was my inconsistency, if that makes sense.
Q5: The video lectures were my lifeline because I'm an auditory learner. I could listen to them while doing other things, replay confusing parts, speed them up when I was cramming. I watched almost five hours worth, which sounds like a lot, but spread across ten courses it was actually my minimum viable engagement. Hearing explanations stuck better than reading ever did.
Q6: I completely ignored the discussion boards—zero posts from me. They felt performative and time-consuming when I was already drowning. Reading materials and PDFs often got downloaded but never opened. If something wasn't going to be directly assessed, I couldn't justify spending my limited energy on it.
Q7: An activity felt worth my time if it directly connected to passing the course or understanding something I'd be tested on. I needed immediate relevance—theoretical discussions or 'enrichment' materials felt like luxuries. If I could see how it would help my exam score or assignment, I'd engage. Otherwise, it went to the bottom of an ever-growing pile.
Q8: Optional activities might as well have been invisible to me. Required work already overwhelmed me, so optional stuff never stood a chance. There was this guilt around them though—I knew they'd probably help, but my brain just couldn't prioritize 'nice to have' over 'must do to not fail.' The anxiety made everything feel urgent except the things that weren't mandatory.
Q9: Absolutely. I downloaded lectures and listened to them on my phone during commutes or while trying to fall asleep. I'd take audio notes, recording myself summarizing concepts and playing them back. A lot of my learning happened through conversations with other students outside the platform—those forty-two peer interactions were just the tip of the iceberg.
Q10: Most of my actual learning happened offline. I'd download a video once, then listen to it five or six times while doing dishes or walking. I made audio recordings of key concepts and played them on repeat. The LMS would show one view, but that content lived in my head for days. My exam score reflects learning that happened entirely outside the system.
Q11: All the time. I'd grab lecture recordings and PDFs in one session, then study from those files for weeks without logging back in. My attendance looks terrible at under eleven percent, but I was engaging with the material—just not in ways the platform could track. It was more efficient for me to have everything locally than to keep navigating the LMS.
Q12: There were several crisis points where everything fell apart. Mid-semester, my anxiety peaked and I could barely function—the CBT sessions helped eventually, but there were weeks where just opening my laptop triggered panic. Family stuff happened, health stuff happened. When you're in survival mode, academic engagement becomes impossible.
Q13: Constantly. Ten courses is genuinely insane, and the workload was crushing. When I was exhausted or confused, I'd log in, stare at the screen, and accomplish nothing. Competing responsibilities meant the LMS was always competing for attention it rarely won. Fatigue made me watch videos at 2x speed without absorbing anything.
Q14: So many times. I'd have the LMS open for hours but be completely checked out mentally. I'd scroll through materials without reading, play videos while my mind wandered elsewhere. My high engagement score doesn't capture that half my 'engagement' was just anxious scrolling or having tabs open while dissociating.
Q15: I postponed assignments constantly, even knowing they were killing my completion rate. The anxiety made starting feel impossible—every task seemed enormous. I'd tell myself I'd do it tomorrow, then tomorrow would come with its own crises. Avoidance became a coping mechanism, even though it made everything worse long-term.
Q16: Deadlines were the only thing that made me engage at all. I'd ignore the LMS for weeks, then frantically log in as due dates approached. My activity probably spiked dramatically around submission times. Without deadlines, I genuinely don't know if I would have engaged at all—they were both my enemy and my only motivator.
Q17: Feedback affected me intensely but not always productively. Good feedback motivated me briefly; critical feedback sent me into anxiety spirals where I'd avoid the course entirely. I needed feedback to improve, but receiving it was emotionally costly. Sometimes I'd delay opening graded work for days because I couldn't handle potentially bad news.
Q18: The structure felt overwhelming with ten courses running simultaneously. There was no way to give each course proper attention, so I triaged constantly. Courses with clearer structure and fewer components were easier to manage. The ones with lots of small tasks scattered throughout just got neglected because tracking everything was impossible.
Q19: Courses with well-organized video content were easier because I could engage auditorily. Courses requiring lots of reading or discussion board participation were harder—they didn't match how I learn. The lack of flexibility in deadlines made things harder too. When everything's due at once across multiple courses, something has to give.
Q20: That's literally me. I logged in constantly, watched tons of videos, interacted with peers, but my assignment completion was abysmal. High activity can mean anxiety-driven checking, research without output, or engagement that doesn't translate to submissions. Sometimes you're learning a lot but can't convert that into the specific format assignments require.
Q21: That also makes sense to me. If someone downloads everything early and studies offline, they'd look inactive but could ace exams. The LMS only sees platform activity, not actual learning. Someone with good time management might need fewer logins to accomplish more than someone like me who's constantly anxious and checking compulsively.
Q22: Logs can show patterns—when you're active, what you click on, time spent. But they miss everything that matters: comprehension, emotional state, offline study, quality of engagement versus quantity. They can't tell if you're actively learning or just having a breakdown with the LMS open. They measure presence, not learning.
Q23: Being engaged means actually caring about and wrestling with the material, not just going through motions. It's when concepts click and you want to understand more. For me, engagement happened in bursts—moments of genuine curiosity between long stretches of survival-mode studying. True engagement is rare and precious, not something you can sustain constantly.
Q24: The LMS can't show my anxiety attacks before logging in, the hours spent psyching myself up to start work, or the genuine learning that happened through audio recordings on walks. It can't capture that my low attendance masked real engagement, or that my high login frequency was often just anxious checking. It misses the human behind the data points.
